danger_signals.visual_form: Bright contrasting colors (typically yellow, red, or white) against darker backgrounds
Sprites moving toward player from edges of screen, growing larger
Large distinctive sprite, often with multiple animated components

danger_signals.meaning: Increased enemy density or difficulty escalation ahead

safety_signals.visual_form: Minimal enemy sprites on screen, open sky areas
Distinctive glowing or pulsing items (weapon upgrades, shields)
Transition screens with reduced sprite activity
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary reprieve and player status display

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Player aircraft design with allied markings
Enemy aircraft with distinct design language, ground-based military structures
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy aircraft types with varying silhouettes and attack patterns
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Fortified enemy installations, industrial/military landscape, urban centers under occupation

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Early campaign - enemy territory gradually encroached
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Skyline features increasing industrial/military structures; more organized enemy formations
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy spawn patterns become more dense and coordinated; boss frequency increases

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Background shows active combat zones, smoke plumes, damaged structures
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies attack in waves; multiple simultaneous threats; power-ups become strategically placed

world_state_to_visual.world_state: Late campaign - pushing toward enemy stronghold
world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy command structures visible; heavily fortified installations; ominous architectural designs
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Boss encounters more frequent; enemies more aggressive; screen scrolling faster

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Victory animation; stage transition screen with campaign progress indicator
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player aircraft returns to safe flight; music changes to transition theme

environmental_storytelling.example: Transition from rural/coastal terrain to industrial/urban enemy-controlled zones communicates territorial progression
Enemy bases feature distinctive blocky, militaristic design versus implied allied structures
Burning structures and smoke plumes indicate ongoing conflict and destruction
Organized enemy formations suggest command hierarchy; disorganized spawns suggest desperation

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Fly in predictable patterns; minimal evasion
Fire projectiles; attempt to intercept player; coordinated with nearby enemies
Dramatic entrance animation; distinctive movement pattern
Multi-phase attack patterns; telegraphed special attacks; larger sprite visible across screen
Weapon output visibly enhanced (larger projectiles, more fire rate); movement speed increased
Slower response; visual sprite may show damage state (depending on implementation)

notes: Steel Empire is a horizontally-scrolling shooter with minimal narrative exposition. World-state communication relies entirely on visual and mechanical progression. The game uses standard shmup visual language: player sprite in blue/cool tones, enemy sprite in red/warm tones, projectiles in contrasting bright colors. Environmental scrolling serves dual purpose of aesthetic storytelling and difficulty escalation. No dialogue or text-based faction communication present; all faction identity conveyed through visual design language of aircraft, installations, and environment. Campaign structure (8 stages) implies linear territorial progression from player-controlled to enemy-held airspace.
